tags: O(1) per test case after reading

content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string SSS. A binary string is a string consisting of only 000's and 111's.

A binary string is called good if it has an equal number of occurrences of 01\texttt{01}01 substrings and 10\texttt{10}10 substrings. Note that these substrings may overlap. For example, the string 110100111010011101001 is good since there are two occurrences of 01\texttt{01}01 (1101‾00111\underline{01}0011101​001 and 1101001‾11010\underline{01}1101001​) and two occurrences of 10\texttt{10}10 (110‾10011\underline{10}1001110​1001 and 11010‾01110\underline{10}0111010​01).

Find the number of indices iii (1≤i≤∣S∣)(1 \le i \le |S|)(1≤i≤∣S∣) such that after flipping SiS_iSi​, the resultant binary string is good. (If SiS_iSi​ is 000, flipping it changes it to 111, and if SiS_iSi​ is 111, flipping it changes it to 000)

**Note:** A string AAA is a substring of a string BBB if AAA can be obtained from BBB by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) characters from the beginning and several (possibly, zero or all) characters from the end.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the number indices iii such that flipping SiS_iSi​ results in a good binary string.
constraints: The sum of |S| over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a binary string S consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the number of indices i (1 ≤ i ≤ |S|) such that flipping the i‑th character of S results in a good string (a string with an equal number of substrings "01" and "10").

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "10100" the first and last characters differ, so only the two ends (positions 1 and 5) work → answer 2. For "11111" the ends are equal and length is 5, so all interior positions (2,3,4) work → answer 5‑2 = 3.

solution.approach:
For any binary string the difference between the number of "01" substrings and the number of "10" substrings depends only on the first and last characters:  diff = (S[0] == '0' ? 1 : 0) - (S[|S|-1] == '0' ? 1 : 0). The string is good iff diff = 0, i.e., the first and last characters are equal. Flipping a character changes only the first or the last character when the flipped position is at an end; interior flips keep both ends unchanged. Therefore:
- If S[0] == S[|S|-1] (already good), every interior position (2 … |S|-1) remains good after flipping, while flipping either end would break the equality. Answer = max(0, |S|-2).
- If S[0] != S[|S|-1] (not good), only flipping an end makes the two ends equal. Both ends are viable, interior positions are not. Answer = 2.
The algorithm reads the string, checks the equality of its first and last characters, and applies the above formula. Time complexity is O(|S|) for reading the input, O(1) for computation per test case; total O(total length). Memory usage is O(1) besides the input string.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total length of all strings) – linear reading, constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (ignoring the input string itself).
